Name: Personal Website
Description: A source-available portfolio site built as a practical ASP.NET Core workbench.
ContributionSummary: Designed and implemented the application architecture, data model, styling system, and deployment path.
Problem: I wanted a personal site that could present projects and writing without becoming a static brochure.
Solution: Build a data-driven ASP.NET Core MVC site with provider-selectable persistence, reusable assets, and structured project metadata.
Result: The site can grow from a portfolio into a small publishing and project showcase platform.
Tradeoffs: The model is more structured than a simple static site, but the structure keeps future content from becoming ad hoc.

Name: Release Dashboard
Description: A test case study for release coordination, status visibility, and handoff hygiene.
ContributionSummary: Modeled release state, surfaced operational checkpoints, and reduced the need to reconstruct status from scattered messages.
Problem: Release work often fails less from one big mistake than from many tiny uncertainties that accumulate between teams.
Solution: Represent the release as explicit state, make ownership visible, and keep common operations close to the people performing them.
Result: The workflow becomes easier to reason about because pending work, completed work, and blocked work are visually distinct.
Tradeoffs: A dashboard can clarify the process, but it can also become another inbox if the data model is not disciplined.

Name: Queue Game Library
Description: A C++ rendering and engine framework experiment centered on queues, ownership, and predictable resource flow.
ContributionSummary: Designed queue-oriented abstractions for rendering work, resource lifetime, and command submission.
Problem: Low-level rendering APIs expose enormous power, but they also make every lifetime and synchronization decision painfully explicit.
Solution: Wrap common engine concerns in familiar C++ abstractions while keeping ownership, performance, and synchronization visible.
Result: The library provides a practical sandbox for exploring engine architecture without pretending the hardware complexity disappears.
Tradeoffs: The abstractions make common work friendlier, but they cannot hide the cost model of the underlying API.

Name: Database Seeding Workbench
Description: A seed-data pipeline for exercising layouts, relationships, and lookup tables during local development.
ContributionSummary: Defined JSON-backed seed data that can populate test databases without duplicating table-specific setup logic.
Problem: A site with empty tables can compile successfully while still hiding layout bugs, relationship bugs, and missing metadata paths.
Solution: Use explicit test records with stable IDs, realistic relationships, and enough variety to stress the pages.
Result: The development database can exercise cards, tag filters, detail pages, post listings, assets, and cross-links.
Tradeoffs: Seed data is intentionally artificial, so it should prove layout behavior without becoming production content.

Name: Content Pipeline Prototype
Description: A data-driven writing pipeline for drafts, revisions, rendered output, and reusable assets.
ContributionSummary: Modeled posts, revisions, tags, render modes, and asset references as first-class data instead of loose files.
Problem: Writing systems become brittle when source text, rendered HTML, metadata, and assets all live in different mental models.
Solution: Separate the authoring source from cached output and keep relationships explicit enough for future rendering strategies.
Result: The site can support short notes now while leaving room for technical posts, galleries, and code-heavy writing later.
Tradeoffs: The model adds tables before the site strictly needs them, but it prevents early shortcuts from becoming permanent architecture.

Name: ZFS Backup Script
Description: A PowerShell-oriented backup script concept for snapshotting datasets and enforcing retention rules.
ContributionSummary: Designed the operational model for repeatable snapshots, retention windows, and safer home-lab maintenance.
Problem: Backups are easy to postpone when the process requires too much manual judgment every time it runs.
Solution: Make the desired retention policy explicit and let the script handle naming, selection, dry runs, and cleanup.
Result: The backup workflow becomes inspectable, repeatable, and less dependent on remembering the right command sequence.
Tradeoffs: Automation reduces routine risk, but destructive cleanup still needs conservative defaults and good visibility.

Name: Direct3D Render Sandbox
Description: A small rendering sandbox used to test pipeline state, asset loading, and frame instrumentation ideas.
ContributionSummary: Experimented with rendering loops, resource setup, and debugging hooks for graphics programming practice.
Problem: Graphics programming rewards experimentation, but throwaway prototypes can become incomprehensible quickly.
Solution: Keep the sandbox intentionally narrow and treat each experiment as a way to validate one rendering concept at a time.
Result: The project serves as a safe place to break assumptions before moving ideas into a cleaner library.
Tradeoffs: The sandbox is useful for learning, but not every experiment deserves to graduate into framework code.

Name: Admin Error Log
Description: An administrative error viewer for paging, inspecting, and triaging website exceptions.
ContributionSummary: Modeled error records and retrieval paths so failures can be inspected without digging through raw hosting logs.
Problem: A production site needs a way to surface failures that is more structured than hoping the right log file is nearby.
Solution: Capture structured error data and expose a paged administrative view with enough context to diagnose common failures.
Result: Operational visibility becomes part of the application instead of an afterthought.
Tradeoffs: Error capture must balance diagnostic detail against privacy, noise, and retention concerns.
